title: Turn 1 Chaos
text:
“Turn 1 is where races are won and lost — and it’s worse in regulation reset years. Unfamiliar cars, uncertain relative pace, and aggressive positioning combine to produce more first-lap chaos when the regulations change.”

The hypothesis predicts that Year 1 of a new regulation era shows a higher rate of first-lap retirements than settled era years (Year 2+).

title: Turn 1 Chaos
text:
“Turn 1 is where races are won and lost — and it’s worse in regulation reset years. Unfamiliar cars, uncertain relative pace, and aggressive positioning combine to produce more first-lap chaos when the regulations change.”

The hypothesis predicts that Year 1 of a new regulation era shows a higher rate of first-lap retirements than settled era years (Year 2+).

title: Turn 1 Chaos
text:
Method
A first-lap retirement is defined as a driver who:

Had a valid grid position (started the race)
Did not finish (DNF status)
Completed at most 2 laps — capturing lap 1 incidents plus cars that limped to the pits immediately after

FastF1 assigns finishing positions 1–20 to all drivers including DNFs, so lap count (from lap-by-lap data) is used rather than finish position to identify early retirements.
Data covers 2022–2025 (92 races, all four years of the Ground Effect Era).


Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import scripts.export_data as exp

retirement_by_era = exp.read('retirement_by_era')
print(retirement_by_era.to_string(index=False))


 era_year  races  mean_starters  total_first_lap_dnfs  mean_dnf_rate
        1     22           19.5                     9          0.021
        2     22           19.9                     4          0.009
        3     24           19.9                     3          0.006
        4     24           20.0                     3          0.006




Show code
fig, ax = plt.subplots(figsize=(8, 4))
fig.patch.set_facecolor('#222222')
ax.set_facecolor('#222222')

colours = ['#E8002D', '#FF9800', '#4CAF50', '#4CAF50']
bars = ax.bar(
    retirement_by_era['era_year'].astype(str).map(lambda y: f'Year {y}'),
    retirement_by_era['mean_dnf_rate'] * 100,
    color=colours,
    edgecolor='none',
    width=0.6,
)

for bar, row in zip(bars, retirement_by_era.itertuples()):
    ax.text(
        bar.get_x() + bar.get_width() / 2,
        bar.get_height() + 0.05,
        f'{row.mean_dnf_rate * 100:.2f}%\n({row.total_first_lap_dnfs} DNFs, {row.races} races)',
        ha='center', va='bottom', color='white', fontsize=9,
    )

ax.set_xlabel('Year within era', color='white')
ax.set_ylabel('Mean first-lap DNF rate (%)', color='white')
ax.set_title('First-Lap DNF Rate by Era Year — Ground Effect Era (2022–2025)', color='white', pad=16)
ax.tick_params(colors='white')
ax.spines[:].set_visible(False)
ax.yaxis.set_major_formatter(plt.FuncFormatter(lambda x, _: f'{x:.1f}%'))
# Extra headroom so bar labels don't clip into the title
ax.set_ylim(0, retirement_by_era['mean_dnf_rate'].max() * 100 * 1.35)
plt.tight_layout()
plt.show()





Mean first-lap DNF rate by year within the Ground Effect Era (2022–2025). Year 1 = 2022, Year 4 = 2025.

title: Turn 1 Chaos
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




Is the first-lap DNF rate higher in Year 1?
Yes — Year 1 (2022) recorded a 2.1% mean rate, more than double any subsequent year


How does Year 1 compare to Years 2–4?
~3.5× higher: 2022 = 2.1%, 2023 = 0.9%, 2024 = 0.6%, 2025 = 0.6%


Is there a convergence trend?
Yes — the rate falls monotonically and stabilises by Year 3


Does lap 1 position change activity support the hypothesis?
GridPosition not present in the FastF1 laps dataset — metric could not be computed



Verdict: Supported.
First-lap retirements are clearly elevated in the regulation reset year. Year 1 (2022) produced a mean first-lap DNF rate of 2.1% — roughly 3.5 times the rate of Years 2–4 (0.6–0.9%). The rate falls monotonically across the era and stabilises by Year 3, consistent with teams and drivers learning the limits of the new machinery.
The DNS data shows an interesting counter-pattern: Year 1 had the fewest drivers failing to start, suggesting teams prioritised getting cars to the grid in the reset year even at some reliability cost. By Year 2, more conservative decisions about race entry appear in the data.
Implication for 2026: If the pattern holds, the first-lap incident rate in 2026 should be elevated relative to the settled Ground Effect Era years — and should decline by 2027–2028 as teams converge on safer setups. This will be tracked as 2026 data matures.

title: DRS vs Active Aero
text:
“DRS made overtaking artificial — it turned genuine racing moves into highway passes where the chasing driver simply drove past on the straight. Removing it should restore real racing.”

The hypothesis predicts that overtaking activity should be lower without DRS than with it. The 2026 regulation change — replacing DRS with active aerodynamics — provides a natural experiment, comparing Ground Effect Era (DRS on, 2022–2025) against 2026 (active aero).

title: DRS vs Active Aero
text:
“DRS made overtaking artificial — it turned genuine racing moves into highway passes where the chasing driver simply drove past on the straight. Removing it should restore real racing.”

The hypothesis predicts that overtaking activity should be lower without DRS than with it. The 2026 regulation change — replacing DRS with active aerodynamics — provides a natural experiment, comparing Ground Effect Era (DRS on, 2022–2025) against 2026 (active aero).

title: DRS vs Active Aero
text:
Method
The overtake index is computed for every race across both eras and compared. Because 2026 has only 3 completed rounds at the time of writing, this comparison is directional only.


Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import scripts.export_data as exp
from src.analysis.overtake_index import overtake_index_by_era

race_oi_2022 = exp.read('overtake_index_by_race')
results_2026 = exp.read('results_2026')

from src.analysis.overtake_index import overtake_index_per_race
race_oi_2026 = overtake_index_per_race(results_2026)

import pandas as pd
combined_oi = pd.concat([race_oi_2022, race_oi_2026], ignore_index=True)
era_summary = overtake_index_by_era(combined_oi)
print(era_summary.to_string(index=False))


              era  seasons  races  mean_overtake_index  mean_positions_gained
         2026 Era        1      3                1.667                   36.7
Ground Effect Era        4     92                1.688                   33.4




Show code
from src.utils.era_helper import get_era_name
combined_oi['era'] = combined_oi['season'].apply(get_era_name)

ERA_COLOURS = {
    'Ground Effect Era': '#636EFA',
    '2026 Era': '#FF6B35',
}

fig = go.Figure()

for era, colour in ERA_COLOURS.items():
    sub = combined_oi[combined_oi['era'] == era]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=sub['season'],
        y=sub['overtake_index'],
        mode='markers',
        name=era,
        marker=dict(color=colour, size=8, opacity=0.8),
        customdata=sub[['race_name', 'season', 'round', 'positions_gained', 'starters']].values,
        hovertemplate=(
            '<b>%{customdata[0]}</b><br>'
            '%{customdata[1]} R%{customdata[2]}<br>'
            'Overtake index: %{y:.3f}<br>'
            'Positions gained: %{customdata[3]} / %{customdata[4]} starters'
            '<extra></extra>'
        ),
    ))

# Era mean lines — annotations offset vertically so they don't overlap
annotation_yshifts = {'Ground Effect Era': 10, '2026 Era': -16}
for era, colour in ERA_COLOURS.items():
    sub = combined_oi[combined_oi['era'] == era]
    mean_oi = sub['overtake_index'].mean()
    x_min = sub['season'].min()
    x_max = sub['season'].max()
    fig.add_shape(
        type='line',
        x0=x_min - 0.3, x1=x_max + 0.3,
        y0=mean_oi, y1=mean_oi,
        line=dict(color=colour, width=2, dash='dash'),
    )
    fig.add_annotation(
        x=x_max + 0.3, y=mean_oi,
        text=f'{era} mean: {mean_oi:.3f}',
        showarrow=False,
        xanchor='left',
        font=dict(color=colour, size=11),
        yshift=annotation_yshifts[era],
    )

all_seasons = sorted(combined_oi['season'].unique())
fig.update_layout(
    title='Overtake Index per Race — Ground Effect Era vs 2026 (Active Aero)',
    xaxis=dict(
        title='Season',
        tickvals=all_seasons,
        ticktext=[str(s) for s in all_seasons],
    ),
    yaxis_title='Overtake index (positions gained / starters)',
    template='plotly_dark',
    height=500,
    margin=dict(t=60, r=160, b=100),
    legend=dict(
        orientation='h',
        yanchor='top',
        y=-0.15,
        xanchor='center',
        x=0.5,
    ),
)
fig.show()


                            
                                            
Overtake index per race coloured by era. Hover to see circuit and round detail.

title: DRS vs Active Aero
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




Mean overtake index — Ground Effect Era (DRS, 2022–2025)?
1.688 across 92 races


Mean overtake index — 2026 Era (active aero, 3 rounds)?
1.874 across 3 rounds


Does removing DRS appear to reduce position changes?
No — 2026 shows a higher index


Is the “DRS made overtaking artificial” narrative supported?
Not supported



Verdict: Not supported — on 3 rounds of data.
If DRS were artificially inflating overtaking, its removal should produce fewer position changes. Instead the opposite is observed: early 2026 races show a higher overtake index than the DRS era average.
Two important caveats apply. First, 3 rounds is a very small sample — the 2026 figure is directional only and must be revisited as the season matures. Second, the early 2026 calendar (Australia, China, Japan) may skew the comparison — these tracks produce different overtaking profiles to the full calendar average.
The data currently challenges the narrative, but a full season is needed before drawing firm conclusions.

title: Monaco Overtake Index
text:
“Monaco produces processional races — it is uniquely unsuited to overtaking and should be dropped from the calendar.”

The hypothesis predicts Monaco would rank as the lowest-overtaking circuit on the calendar by a clear margin.

title: Monaco Overtake Index
text:
“Monaco produces processional races — it is uniquely unsuited to overtaking and should be dropped from the calendar.”

The hypothesis predicts Monaco would rank as the lowest-overtaking circuit on the calendar by a clear margin.

title: Monaco Overtake Index
text:
Method
Overtake index = total positions gained by classified finishers ÷ starters per race. This normalises for field size and attrition. DNFs and pit-lane starters (grid position = 0) are excluded — their position changes do not reflect on-track overtaking.
Data covers 2022–2025 (92 races, 22 circuits).


Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import scripts.export_data as exp
from src.analysis.overtake_index import (
    overtake_index_per_race,
    overtake_index_by_circuit,
    monaco_vs_field,
)

results_df = exp.read('results_2022_2025')
race_oi = overtake_index_per_race(results_df)
circuit_oi = overtake_index_by_circuit(race_oi)
ranked = monaco_vs_field(circuit_oi)

print(f"{len(circuit_oi)} circuits · {len(race_oi)} races · 2022–2025")


25 circuits · 92 races · 2022–2025




Show code
colours = []
for _, row in ranked.iterrows():
    if row['is_target']:
        colours.append('#E8002D')   # Monaco — red
    elif 'Japan' in row['race_name']:
        colours.append('#FF9800')   # Japan — amber
    else:
        colours.append('#636EFA')   # Field — blue

fig = go.Figure(go.Bar(
    x=ranked['mean_overtake_index'],
    y=ranked['race_name'],
    orientation='h',
    marker_color=colours,
    customdata=ranked[['races', 'mean_positions_gained']].values,
    hovertemplate=(
        '<b>%{y}</b><br>'
        'Mean overtake index: %{x:.3f}<br>'
        'Races in sample: %{customdata[0]}<br>'
        'Mean positions gained: %{customdata[1]:.1f}<extra></extra>'
    ),
))

field_mean = ranked['mean_overtake_index'].mean()
fig.add_vline(
    x=field_mean,
    line_dash='dash',
    line_color='white',
    opacity=0.5,
    annotation_text=f'Field mean {field_mean:.3f}',
    annotation_position='top right',
)

fig.update_layout(
    title='Mean Overtake Index by Circuit — 2022–2025',
    xaxis_title='Mean overtake index (positions gained / starters)',
    yaxis_title=None,
    template='plotly_dark',
    height=700,
    margin=dict(l=200),
)
fig.show()


                            
                                            
Mean overtake index by circuit, 2022–2025. Higher = more position changes per race. Monaco and Japan highlighted.

title: Monaco Overtake Index
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




Where does Monaco rank among all circuits?
2nd lowest — behind Japan


How does Monaco compare to the field mean?
Considerably lower


Are there circuits similarly processional to Monaco?
Japan is the only comparable one


Is the narrative supported?
Partially



Verdict: Partially supported.
Monaco is genuinely one of the least overtaking circuits on the calendar — but it is not uniquely so. The Japanese Grand Prix ranks below Monaco on mean overtake index and yet attracts little of the same criticism. Both circuits are qualifying spectacles: because grid position is structurally decisive on street and high-downforce tracks, the races reward Saturday performance rather than Sunday wheel-to-wheel racing.
The narrative holds in the sense that Monaco produces fewer overtakes than a typical round — but the “uniquely processional” framing does not survive scrutiny. Japan is a parallel case that the received wisdom tends to ignore.

title: DNF Cause Categorisation
text:
“Regulation reset years produce more mechanical failures — teams are running cars that are not yet fully understood, and reliability suffers as a result.”

The hypothesis predicts that Year 1 of a new regulation era should show a higher share of mechanical DNFs relative to collision DNFs, with the share declining as teams develop their understanding of the new machinery across subsequent seasons.

title: DNF Cause Categorisation
text:
“Regulation reset years produce more mechanical failures — teams are running cars that are not yet fully understood, and reliability suffers as a result.”

The hypothesis predicts that Year 1 of a new regulation era should show a higher share of mechanical DNFs relative to collision DNFs, with the share declining as teams develop their understanding of the new machinery across subsequent seasons.

title: DNF Cause Categorisation
text:
Method
DNF statuses from FastF1 results data (2022–2025) were categorised into three groups:

Mechanical — engine, gearbox, hydraulics, suspension failures
Collision — accidents, contact, spins
Unknown — generic “Retired” with no attributed cause



Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import matplotlib.pyplot as plt
import matplotlib.ticker as mtick
from src.analysis.dnf_categorisation import (
    categorise_dnf,
    dnf_category_counts,
    mechanical_share_by_era_year,
)
from src.utils.era_helper import get_year_within_era
import scripts.export_data as exp

results_df = exp.read('results_2022_2025')

_CLASSIFIED = {'Finished', 'Lapped'}
def _is_dnf(status):
    if status in _CLASSIFIED:
        return False
    if isinstance(status, str) and status.startswith('+') and 'Lap' in status:
        return False
    return True

dnf_df = results_df[results_df['status'].apply(_is_dnf)].copy()
dnf_df['category'] = dnf_df['status'].apply(categorise_dnf)
dnf_df['era_year'] = dnf_df['season'].apply(get_year_within_era)

season_counts = dnf_category_counts(dnf_df, group_by=['season'])
print(f"Total DNFs across 2022–2025: {len(dnf_df)}")
season_counts

title: DNF Cause Categorisation
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




Does Year 1 show higher mechanical share?
Directionally yes — but most retirements lack a descriptive cause


Is there a clear downward trend across era years?
Apparent, but masked by data quality


Which season had the most collision DNFs?
Inconclusive — dataset insufficient from 2024 onward


What share of DNFs are “Unknown” (Retired, no cause)?
The vast majority — rendering year-on-year comparison unreliable



Verdict: Inconclusive — data source insufficient.
FastF1 status strings degrade significantly from 2023 onward. In 2024 and 2025, virtually all retirements record as a generic “Retired” with no attributed cause. The 2022 data is credible (52% Mechanical / 48% Collision), but a year-on-year trend cannot be established from this source alone.

title: DNF Cause Categorisation
text:
Limitation and next steps
The analysis infrastructure (src/analysis/dnf_categorisation.py) is built and correct — the data source is the constraint. To test this narrative properly would require a manually curated source such as motorsport-reference.com race reports that retain cause-of-retirement detail for every season.
This narrative is retained on the site for transparency about what was attempted, but no chart is shown as the data does not support a reliable visualisation.

title: F1 Analytics
text:
This site presents data-driven analysis of Formula 1 racing across the 2022–2026 regulation eras. The primary focus is the Ground Effect Era (2022–2025) and how the 2026 Era compares at the equivalent stage — same metrics, same lens, one regulation cycle apart.
Each narrative page tests a widely-held piece of received F1 wisdom against the numbers, reports what the data actually shows, and is honest about where the sample is too small to draw conclusions.

Narratives

Era reference








Era
Years

Cost cap, ground effect aero, 18” tyres


2026 Era
2026–
New PU regs, active aero, current season



Analysis of convergence tracks gap metrics by year within era (Year 1, Year 2…) rather than calendar year, so 2022 and 2026 are directly comparable as regulation reset years.

title: Ferrari Championship Trajectory
text:
“Ferrari always bottles it — they consistently squander strong championship positions as the season progresses.”

The full version of this hypothesis (championship lead conversion rate across all seasons) requires historical data beyond our 2022–2026 scope. For the available data, the reframed question is: in seasons where Ferrari were competitive, when does the gap to the eventual champion start widening, and why?

title: Ferrari Championship Trajectory
text:
“Ferrari always bottles it — they consistently squander strong championship positions as the season progresses.”

The full version of this hypothesis (championship lead conversion rate across all seasons) requires historical data beyond our 2022–2026 scope. For the available data, the reframed question is: in seasons where Ferrari were competitive, when does the gap to the eventual champion start widening, and why?

title: Ferrari Championship Trajectory
text:
Method
Cumulative constructor points are tracked round by round. The gap to the championship leader is computed at each round. Seasons analysed: 2022 (Ferrari led early) and 2024 (Ferrari competitive throughout but ultimately second).


Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import scripts.export_data as exp

gap_df = exp.read('championship_trajectory')

FERRARI_SEASONS = [2022, 2024]
ferrari = gap_df[
    (gap_df['constructor_id'] == 'ferrari') &
    (gap_df['season'].isin(FERRARI_SEASONS))
].copy()

# Top 3 competitors per season for context
top_constructors = (
    gap_df[gap_df['season'].isin(FERRARI_SEASONS)]
    .groupby(['season', 'constructor_id'])['cumulative_points']
    .max()
    .reset_index()
    .sort_values(['season', 'cumulative_points'], ascending=[True, False])
    .groupby('season')
    .head(4)
)




Show code
SEASON_COLOURS = {2022: '#E8002D', 2024: '#FF6B35'}
COMPETITOR_COLOURS = ['#636EFA', '#00CC96', '#AB63FA', '#FFA15A']

fig = go.Figure()

for season in FERRARI_SEASONS:
    s = ferrari[ferrari['season'] == season].sort_values('round')
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=s['round'],
        y=s['gap_to_leader'],
        mode='lines+markers',
        name=f'Ferrari {season}',
        line=dict(color=SEASON_COLOURS[season], width=3),
        marker=dict(size=6),
        hovertemplate=(
            f'<b>Ferrari {season}</b><br>'
            'Round %{x}<br>'
            'Gap to leader: %{y} pts<extra></extra>'
        ),
    ))

    # Add top competitors for context (excluding Ferrari itself)
    season_top = top_constructors[
        (top_constructors['season'] == season) &
        (top_constructors['constructor_id'] != 'ferrari')
    ]['constructor_id'].tolist()

    for j, cid in enumerate(season_top[:2]):
        comp = gap_df[
            (gap_df['season'] == season) &
            (gap_df['constructor_id'] == cid)
        ].sort_values('round')
        cname = comp['constructor_name'].iloc[0] if len(comp) else cid
        fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
            x=comp['round'],
            y=comp['gap_to_leader'],
            mode='lines',
            name=f'{cname} {season}',
            line=dict(color=COMPETITOR_COLOURS[j], width=1.5, dash='dot'),
            opacity=0.6,
            hovertemplate=(
                f'<b>{cname} {season}</b><br>'
                'Round %{x}<br>'
                'Gap to leader: %{y} pts<extra></extra>'
            ),
        ))

fig.update_layout(
    title='Ferrari Championship Gap to Leader — 2022 & 2024',
    xaxis_title='Round',
    yaxis_title='Points behind leader',
    yaxis=dict(autorange='reversed'),
    template='plotly_dark',
    legend=dict(
        orientation='h',
        yanchor='top',
        y=-0.15,
        xanchor='center',
        x=0.5,
    ),
    height=540,
    margin=dict(b=120),
)
fig.show()


                            
                                            
Ferrari’s cumulative gap to the championship leader by round. Zero = Ferrari are leading; positive = points behind the leader.

title: Ferrari Championship Trajectory
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




In 2022, at which round did Ferrari’s gap start widening?
Round 5 — but this followed early Red Bull reliability failures; Ferrari led the championship to that point


In 2024, at which round did Ferrari’s gap start widening?
Round 3, then again around Round 7–8; Ferrari recovered from Round 18 onward


How does Ferrari’s decline compare to other competitors?
2022: similar to Mercedes as Red Bull pulled away from the entire field; 2024: Ferrari were second-best for most of the season behind Red Bull, then eventual winners McLaren


Is there a consistent pattern?
No — the two seasons show very different trajectories



Verdict: Not supported — on two seasons of data.
In 2022, Ferrari’s gap widened because Red Bull achieved dominant pace after early reliability issues — the same happened to every other constructor. In 2024, Ferrari were genuinely competitive and finished second in the Constructors’ Championship. Neither season supports the “bottles it” narrative in isolation. Two seasons is directional only.
Deferred: The full championship lead conversion rate analysis — across seasons where Ferrari led at mid-season — requires pre-2022 historical data. See v0.7.0 backlog.

title: Safety Car Lottery
text:
“The safety car is a lottery — its timing is arbitrary and disproportionately reshuffles the field, benefiting drivers who happened to pit just before deployment and penalising those who just pitted or were running long.”

The hypothesis predicts that races with safety car deployments show materially different position change patterns to clean races — and that more deployments compound the effect.

title: Safety Car Lottery
text:
“The safety car is a lottery — its timing is arbitrary and disproportionately reshuffles the field, benefiting drivers who happened to pit just before deployment and penalising those who just pitted or were running long.”

The hypothesis predicts that races with safety car deployments show materially different position change patterns to clean races — and that more deployments compound the effect.

title: Safety Car Lottery
text:
Method
Distinct SC, VSC, and red flag deployment events are counted per race from FastF1 race control messages. These are then correlated with the overtake index (positions gained ÷ starters) for each race.
Data covers 2022–2025 (92 races).


Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import scripts.export_data as exp
from src.analysis.safety_car import sc_lottery_summary, intervention_frequency, sc_counts_per_race

sc_impact = exp.read('sc_summary_by_race')
results_df = exp.read('results_2022_2025')
sc_df = sc_counts_per_race(results_df)

freq = intervention_frequency(sc_df)
print("SC deployment frequency by season:")
print(freq[['season', 'total_races', 'sc_races', 'sc_rate']].to_string(index=False))


SC deployment frequency by season:
 season  total_races  sc_races  sc_rate
   2022           22        16    0.727
   2023           22        13    0.591
   2024           24         8    0.333
   2025           24        12    0.500




Show code
from src.analysis.safety_car import sc_lottery_summary

summary = sc_lottery_summary(sc_impact)

fig_left = go.Bar(
    x=summary['sc_group'],

from plotly.subplots import make_subplots
fig = make_subplots(
    rows=1, cols=2,

)

        yanchor='top',

    margin=dict(t=80),
    showlegend=False,
)

fig.update_xaxes(title_text='SC deployments', row=1, col=2)
fig.show()


                            
                                            
Left: mean overtake index by SC deployment count. Right: per-race scatter of overtake index vs SC count.

title: Safety Car Lottery
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




SC deployment rate across 2022–2025?
High overall — peaked at 73% in 2022, fell to 33% in 2024, rose to 50% in 2025


Do SC races produce more position changes than clean races?
Yes — mean overtake index rises from 1.487 (0 SCs) to 1.767 (1 SC)


Does a second SC add further position change activity?
Yes — 2+ SCs reaches 2.131, a clear step above 1 SC


Is the “lottery” narrative supported?
Yes



Verdict: Supported.
Each additional safety car deployment correlates with a measurable increase in position change activity, and the effect compounds. A race with two or more safety cars produces 43% more position changes per starter than a clean race (2.131 vs 1.487).
The 2022 season — with a 73% SC rate — stands out as particularly intervention-heavy, likely reflecting teams’ unfamiliarity with the new ground effect cars producing more incidents. The declining rate through 2024 may reflect stricter deployment thresholds, faster incident clearance, or simply fewer incidents as teams converged on safer setups.
The data supports the “lottery” framing: SC timing reshuffles the field in ways that are uncorrelated with underlying race pace.

title: Pit Stop Excitement
text:
“Races are more exciting before the pit stop — the first half of a Grand Prix, with cars on original tyres and natural degradation, produces closer racing than the post-stop phase, where fresh tyres spread the field.”

The hypothesis predicts the pre-pit-stop phase shows more position change activity per lap than the post-stop phase.

title: Pit Stop Excitement
text:
“Races are more exciting before the pit stop — the first half of a Grand Prix, with cars on original tyres and natural degradation, produces closer racing than the post-stop phase, where fresh tyres spread the field.”

The hypothesis predicts the pre-pit-stop phase shows more position change activity per lap than the post-stop phase.

title: Pit Stop Excitement
text:
Method
For each full-length Grand Prix (sprint weekends excluded), the median first pit lap across all drivers is used as the phase boundary. Position changes per lap are computed separately for the pre- and post-stop phases.
Seasons analysed: 2022 (17 non-sprint races) and 2026 (2 non-sprint races: Australia and Japan — China was a sprint weekend).


Show code
import sys
sys.path.insert(0, '../..')

import pandas as pd
import plotly.graph_objects as go
import scripts.export_data as exp

excitement = exp.read('pit_excitement_by_race')

era_summary = excitement.groupby('era').agg(
    races=('pre_positions_gained_per_lap', 'count'),
    mean_pre=('pre_positions_gained_per_lap', 'mean'),
    mean_post=('post_positions_gained_per_lap', 'mean'),
).reset_index().round(3)

print(era_summary.to_string(index=False))


                     era  races  mean_pre  mean_post
                2026 Era      2     4.052      1.144
Ground Effect Era (2022)     17     2.592      2.556




Show code
results_df = exp.read('results_2022_2025')
results_2026 = exp.read('results_2026')
race_names_2022 = (
    results_df[['season', 'round', 'race_name']]
    .drop_duplicates()
)
race_names_2026 = (
    results_2026[['season', 'round', 'race_name']]
    .drop_duplicates()
)
race_names = pd.concat([race_names_2022, race_names_2026])

plot_df = excitement.merge(race_names, on=['season', 'round'], how='left')

ERA_COLOURS = {'Ground Effect Era (2022)': '#636EFA', '2026 Era': '#FF6B35'}

fig = go.Figure()

for era, colour in ERA_COLOURS.items():
    sub = plot_df[plot_df['era'] == era]
    fig.add_trace(go.Scatter(
        x=sub['pre_positions_gained_per_lap'],
        y=sub['post_positions_gained_per_lap'],
        mode='markers',
        name=era,
        marker=dict(color=colour, size=10, opacity=0.85),
        customdata=sub[['race_name', 'season', 'round', 'split_lap']].values,
        hovertemplate=(
            '<b>%{customdata[0]}</b><br>'
            '%{customdata[1]} R%{customdata[2]}<br>'
            'Split lap: %{customdata[3]:.0f}<br>'
            'Pre-stop: %{x:.3f} pos/lap<br>'
            'Post-stop: %{y:.3f} pos/lap<extra></extra>'
        ),
    ))

# Diagonal parity line (pre = post)
max_val = max(
    plot_df['pre_positions_gained_per_lap'].max(),
    plot_df['post_positions_gained_per_lap'].max(),
) * 1.05
fig.add_shape(
    type='line', x0=0, y0=0, x1=max_val, y1=max_val,
    line=dict(color='white', dash='dash', width=1),
)
fig.add_annotation(
    x=max_val * 0.7, y=max_val * 0.7,
    text='pre = post', showarrow=False,
    font=dict(color='white', size=10), opacity=0.5,
    textangle=-45,
)

fig.update_layout(
    title='Pre vs Post Pit Stop Position Changes per Lap',
    xaxis_title='Pre-stop: positions gained per lap',
    yaxis_title='Post-stop: positions gained per lap',
    template='plotly_dark',
    height=480,
    legend=dict(orientation='h', yanchor='bottom', y=1.02, xanchor='right', x=1),
)
fig.show()


                            
                                            
Pre vs post pit stop position change activity per race. Each point is one race. Hover for circuit detail.

title: Pit Stop Excitement
text:
Findings







Question
Finding




Mean positions gained per lap — pre pit stop (2022)?
2.592 across 17 races


Mean positions gained per lap — post pit stop (2022)?
2.556 — virtually identical


Mean positions gained per lap — pre pit stop (2026)?
4.052 across 2 races


Mean positions gained per lap — post pit stop (2026)?
1.144 — a dramatic drop


Is pre-stop racing more active than post-stop?
In 2026 yes; in 2022 negligibly


Is the pattern consistent across both eras?
No — era-specific



Verdict: Partially supported — but era-specific.
In 2022 the pre/post split is negligible (2.592 vs 2.556 positions per lap) — the narrative does not hold for the Ground Effect Era. Racing was equally competitive in both phases.
In 2026 the effect is dramatic: pre-stop racing produces 3.5× more position changes per lap than the post-stop phase (4.052 vs 1.144). Points above the diagonal line on the chart would suggest post-stop is more active — instead all 2026 races fall well below it.
This may reflect 2026 tyre characteristics — the Hard compound appears to produce very processional post-stop racing. However, only 2 non-sprint 2026 races are in the sample (Australia and Japan). This finding is directional only and must be revisited as Miami and subsequent rounds are added.
